title: Basics of Philosophy — Socrates Onward
mode: discussion / conceptual, research-grounded (Wikipedia-first grounding per topic and per lesson; no delegated build unless scoped later)

lifecycleNotes.taught: Socratic session complete; every checklist item demonstrated
lifecycleNotes.spec'd: HANDOFF.md written (delegated builds only) — not expected for this topic
lifecycleNotes.built: not applicable unless a delegated build is scoped later
lifecycleNotes.done: all required artifacts exist and verified on disk

gates: a lesson may not be marked done without its required artifacts on disk
a lesson is taught FROM its grounding (topic-scope research/ first, then lesson-scope) — claims trace to gathered sources, not model recall

grounding.method: Wikipedia-first, branch outward (SEP/IEP, primary texts) per lesson and per topic; gathered via parallel subagents

cursor.nextAction: 102-plato grounded (research/Plato branch gate green). Teaching in progress: Socratic loop from the grounding, one raw-notes entry per exchange.

lessons.title: Socrates — the examined life and the birth of moral philosophy
lessons.motivator: Start the philosophy series at its conventional hinge: the figure who turned inquiry from nature to how one should live, and whose method (questioning) is also this project's teaching method.
lessons.feedsForward: Sets up Plato (his student and chief source), the Socratic problem as a lesson in evidence, and the series arc toward Aristotle and the Hellenistic schools.

lessons.title: Plato — the Forms, the Republic, and the first system
lessons.motivator: Socrates asked 'What is X?' and died without answers; Plato is the student who built the first full answer — Forms, the divided soul, the just city — and founded the school that carried it.
lessons.feedsForward: Sets up Aristotle (trained in, then rebelling against, the Forms), resolves the 101 akrasia debate via the tripartite soul, and plants the Neoplatonism -> Augustine hinge for later in the arc.
gotchas: Learner holds 'akrasia is real' from 101 — the tripartite soul is Plato agreeing with them; use it as vindication, not correction.
Learner's standing 'language too fuzzy / logic too rigid' thesis maps onto doxa vs. episteme — pull that thread.
Learner identifies with Protagoras/contrarians; Popper's anti-Plato case is a natural hook for the politics section.

parked: pre-Socratics as an optional backfill lesson if the grounding suggests it
Hellenistic schools (Stoics, Epicureans, Skeptics) — sequence after the grounding research
